prompt: What are the four standard sections of an Architecture Decision Record (ADR)?
Which of the following are genuine benefits of writing decisions down in ADRs? (Select all that apply.)
choices: It records why a choice was made, so the team doesn't re-argue settled decisions
It forces an honest look at the downsides of the chosen option
It guarantees the decision will never need to change
It helps new team members understand why the system is the way it is

prompt: A teammate documents an option by listing only its advantages and leaves the downsides blank. Why is this a problem?
choices: ADRs are not allowed to mention advantages
An honest trade-off must state what you give up, not only what you gain
Every option must always be rejected
The Consequences section is optional and can be skipped

prompt: Six months after a decision, the team realises the context has changed and the choice no longer fits. What is the recommended way to handle this with ADRs?
choices: Delete the old ADR so no one is confused
Edit the old ADR's decision in place and remove the original reasoning
Write a new ADR that supersedes the old one and references it
Avoid changing anything, since the decision was already recorded
